message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Pre-OS Boot: Component Firmware technique to modify firmware on system components, enabling persistent access and evasion of host-based defenses. Unlike system firmware or BIOS, component firmware often lacks robust integrity checks, making it a target for sophisticated attacks. Malicious firmware can execute code outside the operating system, surviving hard disk re-images and typical system failures. Notable examples include Cyclops Blink, which patches legitimate firmware on devices like WatchGuard, and Equation, which can overwrite hard drive firmware from certain manufacturers. Mitigation strategies include employing hardware-backed security features, regularly updating firmware, and using firmware integrity monitoring tools to detect unauthorized modifications.
